_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Resuscitation: Cardiac Arrest (VF/VT/Asystole/PEA). NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Resuscitation: Cardiac Arrest (VF/VT/Asystole/PEA)

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Resuscitation: Cardiac Arrest (VF/VT/Asystole/PEA)
points: Return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC)
Preservation of neurologic function
High-quality chest compressions/CPR with minimal interruption from recognition of cardiac arrest until confirmation of ROSC or field termination of care

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patients with cardiac arrest
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Patients suffering cardiac arrest due to severe hypothermia [See Hypothermia/Cold Exposure Guideline]
Patients with identifiable Do Not Resuscitate (or equivalent such as POLST) order [See Do Not Resuscitate Status/Advance Directive/Healthcare Power of Attorney (POA) Status Guideline]
Patients in arrest due to traumatic etiology [See General Trauma Management Guideline]

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: High-quality CPR: 100-120 compressions/min, 2-inch depth, allow full recoil
AED — apply as soon as available; minimize pause in compressions
BVM ventilation: 30:2 until advanced airway placed, then 1 breath/6 sec
Control external hemorrhage if traumatic mechanism
Epinephrine auto-injector if anaphylactic cause

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Supraglottic airway (LMA/King) for airway management
CPAP/BVM with PEEP if prearrest respiratory failure
Mechanical CPR device (LUCAS, AutoPulse) when available
12-lead ECG acquisition to identify reversible causes (hyperkalemia, STEMI)
IV access maintenance (existing access only)
Oral glucose if hypoglycemic cause suspected

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IO access initiation for medication and fluid delivery
Peripheral IV access initiation
Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO q 3-5 min (standard arrest dose)
IV/IO fluid resuscitation if hypovolemic cause suspected
IV D50W or dextrose for hypoglycemic cause
IV naloxone for opioid-induced cardiac arrest
Waveform capnography for CPR quality feedback (EtCO2 >20 mmHg suggests effective CPR; >40 mmHg suggests ROSC)

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Endotracheal intubation: continuous waveform capnography after placement
Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO (VF/pVT shock-refractory); 150 mg additional dose
Lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg IV/IO as alternative to amiodarone
Sodium bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg IV/IO for prolonged arrest, hyperkalemia, TCA overdose
Calcium chloride 1 g IV/IO for hyperkalemia or CCB overdose
ROSC: post-ROSC care protocol — targeted temperature management, avoid hypoxia/hypotension
Transcutaneous pacing for PEA with bradycardia mechanism
Point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) to identify reversible causes (pneumothorax, tamponade)

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Performing manual chest compressions in a moving vehicle may pose a clinician safety concern
In addition, manual chest compressions during patient movement are less effective in regard to hands on time, depth, recoil and rate
Ideally, patients should be resuscitated as close to the scene as operationally possible
Risks and benefits should be considered before patient movement in cardiac arrest situations

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Effective chest compressions and defibrillation are the most important therapies to the patient in cardiac arrest. Effective chest compressions are defined as: a. A rate of greater than 100 and less than 120 compressions/minute

Avoid excessive ventilation and consider delayed airway management ' If no advanced airway, consider: a. Passive ventilation using an NRB with 3'4 cycles of uninterrupted chest compressions (for arrests of suspected cardiac etiology). Consider BVM ventilation or advanced airway after 3'4 cycles

Quantitative end-tidal capnography (EtCO2) should be used to monitor effectiveness of chest compressions a. If EtCO2 less than 10 mmHg during the initial phases of resuscitation, attempt to improve chest compression quality

Chest compressions are usually the most rapidly applied therapy for the patient in cardiac arrest and should be initiated as soon as the patient is noted to be pulseless. If the patient is being monitored with pads in place at the time of arrest, immediate defibrillation should take precedence over all other therapies. However, if there is any delay in defibrillation (e.g., in order to place pads), chest compressions should be initiated while the defibrillator is being applied. There is no guidance on how long these initial compressions should be applied; however, it is reasonable to either complete between 30 seconds and 2 minutes of chest

There is insufficient evidence to recommend the routine use of extracorporeal CPR (ECPR) for patients with cardiac arrest. In settings where it can be rapidly implemented, ECPR may be considered for select cardiac arrest patients for whom the suspected etiology of the cardiac arrest is potentially reversible during a limited period of mechanical cardiorespiratory support
Chest compressions should be reinitiated immediately after defibrillation as pulses, if present, are often difficult to detect and rhythm and pulse checks interrupt compressions
Continue chest compressions between completion of AED analysis and AED charging

The effectiveness of chest compressions decreases when moving patients a. Patients should therefore be resuscitated as close to the point at which they are first encountered and should only be moved if the conditions on scene are unsafe or do not operationally allow for resuscitation

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Should be tailored to any locally utilized data registry but may include as a minimum the following elements: o Resuscitation attempted and all interventions performed
o Arrest witnessed
o Location of arrest
o First monitored rhythm
o CPR before EMS arrival
o Presumed etiology . Presumed cardiac
Respiratory
Other non-cardiac

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Risks and benefits should be considered before patient movement in cardiac arrest situations
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
